([/hoʊlt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born 7 December 1989) is an English actor. [His filmography](/wiki/List_of_roles_and_awards_of_Nicholas_Hoult "List of roles and awards of Nicholas Hoult") includes supporting work in big-budget mainstream productions and starring roles in [independent projects](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") in American and British films. He has received several accolades, including nominations for a [British Academy Film Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Award "British Academy Film Award"), two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), and a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award"). He was included in [*Forbes* 30 Under 30](/wiki/Forbes_30_Under_30 "Forbes 30 Under 30") in 2012.

**Nicholas Caradoc Hoult** ([/hoʊlt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); born 7 December 1989) is an English actor. [His filmography](/wiki/List_of_roles_and_awards_of_Nicholas_Hoult "List of roles and awards of Nicholas Hoult") includes supporting work in big-budget mainstream productions and starring roles in [independent projects](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") in American and British films. He has received several accolades, including nominations for a [British Academy Film Award](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Award "British Academy Film Award"), two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), and a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award"). He was included in [*Forbes* 30 Under 30](/wiki/Forbes_30_Under_30 "Forbes 30 Under 30") in 2012.

Hoult performed in local theatre productions as a child. He made his screen debut aged six, in the 1996 film *[Intimate Relations](/wiki/Intimate_Relations_(1996_film) "Intimate Relations (1996 film)")*, and appeared in several television programmes. His breakthrough came with his role in the 2002 comedy-drama *[About a Boy](/wiki/About_a_Boy_(film) "About a Boy (film)")*. He achieved wider recognition for his performance as [Tony Stonem](/wiki/Tony_Stonem "Tony Stonem") in the [E4](/wiki/E4_(TV_channel) "E4 (TV channel)") teen series *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")* (2007–2008). His transition to adult roles began with the 2009 drama *[A Single Man](/wiki/A_Single_Man "A Single Man")* and the fantasy film *[Clash of the Titans](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film) "Clash of the Titans (2010 film)")* (2010). He played the mutant [Hank McCoy / Beast](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)") in the 2011 superhero film *[X-Men: First Class](/wiki/X-Men:_First_Class "X-Men: First Class")*, a role he reprised in later instalments of the film series.

Hoult played the [title role](/wiki/Jack_(hero) "Jack (hero)") in the adventure film *[Jack the Giant Slayer](/wiki/Jack_the_Giant_Slayer "Jack the Giant Slayer")* (2013) and the [zombie](/wiki/Zombie "Zombie") lead in the [Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet "Romeo and Juliet") homage comedy *[Warm Bodies](/wiki/Warm_Bodies_(film) "Warm Bodies (film)")* (2013).

Hoult had supporting roles in the action film *[Mad Max: Fury Road](/wiki/Mad_Max:_Fury_Road "Mad Max: Fury Road")* (2015), portrayed various historical figures such as [Robert Harley](/wiki/Robert_Harley,_1st_Earl_of_Oxford_and_Earl_Mortimer "Robert Harley, 1st Earl of Oxford and Earl Mortimer") in the black comedy *[The Favourite](/wiki/The_Favourite_(2018_film) "The Favourite (2018 film)")* (2018) and [Peter III, Emperor of Russia](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Russia "Peter III of Russia") in the [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") comedy-drama series *[The Great](/wiki/The_Great_(TV_series) "The Great (TV series)")* (2020–2023). His work on *The Great* earned him nominations for two Golden Globes and a Primetime Emmy Award. He has since starred in the black comedy *[The Menu](/wiki/The_Menu_(2022_film) "The Menu (2022 film)")* (2022), the courtroom drama *[Juror #2](/wiki/Juror_No._2 "Juror No. 2")* (2024), the horror film *[Nosferatu](/wiki/Nosferatu_(2024_film) "Nosferatu (2024 film)")* (2024), and the superhero film *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_(2025_film) "Superman (2025 film)")* (2025).

Outside of film, Hoult voiced Elliot in the 2010 [action role-playing game](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game") *[Fable III](/wiki/Fable_III "Fable III")* and appeared in the 2009 [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") play *New Boy*. He supports the charitable organisations [Teenage Cancer Trust](/wiki/Teenage_Cancer_Trust "Teenage Cancer Trust") and [Christian Aid](/wiki/Christian_Aid "Christian Aid"). He is also a trained racing driver, competing in the [Ferrari Challenege](/wiki/Ferrari_Challenge "Ferrari Challenge") racing series.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Nicholas Caradoc Hoult was born on 7 December 1989,[[2]](#cite_note-2) in [Wokingham](/wiki/Wokingham "Wokingham"), Berkshire, England,[[3]](#cite_note-buddytv3-3) to Glenis (née Brown), a piano teacher, and Roger Hoult, a commercial pilot.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) His middle name, Caradoc (pronounced /ka.ˈra.dɔk/), originates from [Middle Welsh](/wiki/Middle_Welsh "Middle Welsh") and translates to "beloved one."[[6]](#cite_note-6) His paternal great-aunt, [Anna Neagle](/wiki/Anna_Neagle "Anna Neagle"), was a renowned stage and film actress, celebrated during the 1930s and 1940s.[[7]](#cite_note-Clarke-2015-7) Hoult has three siblings: an older brother and two sisters.[[3]](#cite_note-buddytv3-3)[[8]](#cite_note-fisher-8)

Hoult spent much of his early years in the village of [Sindlesham](/wiki/Sindlesham "Sindlesham"), located in the borough of Wokingham, where his family lived on a quiet estate.[[9]](#cite_note-9) His older siblings were passionate about acting and dancing from a young age, participating in classes and auditions. As a child, Hoult often accompanied them to these events, which sparked his own interest in acting. Reflecting on his childhood in a 2011 interview with *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, he described his upbringing as "pretty outdoorsy" and "normal,"[[10]](#cite_note-Adel-2011-10) noting that he and his siblings enjoyed "running around in the garden and making tree houses."[[10]](#cite_note-Adel-2011-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Nicholas_Hoult_at_the_2024_Toronto_International_Film_Festival_5.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Hoult_at_the_2024_Toronto_International_Film_Festival_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Hoult_at_the_2024_Toronto_International_Film_Festival_5.jpg)

Nicholas Hoult, at the 2024 [Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival") (TIFF) for the movie *The Order*.

He attended the Coombes Infant and Nursery School before moving on to Arborfield Church of England Junior School for his primary education.[[11]](#cite_note-11) He also developed a passion for ballet during his early years and performed as a dancer with several high-profile companies, participating in regional productions. He notably took part in prestigious productions of *[Swan Lake](/wiki/Swan_Lake "Swan Lake")* and *[The Nutcracker](/wiki/The_Nutcracker "The Nutcracker")* with the [English National Ballet](/wiki/English_National_Ballet "English National Ballet"), showcasing his versatility and dedication to the performing arts.[[12]](#cite_note-12) However, in 2002, at the age of 12, Hoult decided to shift his focus towards acting and enrolled at the renowned [Sylvia Young Theatre School](/wiki/Sylvia_Young_Theatre_School "Sylvia Young Theatre School"), marking the beginning of his formal training in the craft.[[13]](#cite_note-sylvia2-13)

At the age of 14, Hoult left his previous school and attended [Ranelagh School](/wiki/Ranelagh_School "Ranelagh School"),[[14]](#cite_note-The-Daily-Telegraph-2009-14) a [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")'s secondary school in Bracknell, Berkshire, where he continued his education while balancing his growing interest in acting.[[14]](#cite_note-The-Daily-Telegraph-2009-14) By 2006, he made the decision to further his studies at [Farnborough Sixth Form College in Hampshire](/wiki/Sixth_Form_College,_Farnborough "Sixth Form College, Farnborough"), where he studied [A-levels](/wiki/A-level "A-level") in English literature, biology, and psychology.[[8]](#cite_note-fisher-8) After filming the first season of *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")*, he chose to leave his studies behind and dedicate himself fully to acting.[[8]](#cite_note-fisher-8)

Apart from his acting pursuits, Hoult also had a musical background. As a child, he played the [trombone](/wiki/Trombone "Trombone") and was an active member of his local [choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir").[[3]](#cite_note-buddytv3-3) Hoult played [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") for the [Reading Rockets](/wiki/Reading_Rockets "Reading Rockets"), who played in the [English Basketball League](/wiki/English_Basketball_League "English Basketball League").[[15]](#cite_note-Fort-2015-15) He was later appointed as the club's ambassador.[[15]](#cite_note-Fort-2015-15)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

See also: [List of Nicholas Hoult performances](/wiki/List_of_Nicholas_Hoult_performances "List of Nicholas Hoult performances")

### 1996–2005: Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1996–2005: Early career")]

Hoult's acting potential was discovered at the age of three, by a theatre director during a performance of a play that starred Hoult's brother. The director was impressed at Hoult's ability to "concentrate well" and offered him a role in his next theatre production *[The Caucasian Chalk Circle](/wiki/The_Caucasian_Chalk_Circle "The Caucasian Chalk Circle")*.[[14]](#cite_note-The-Daily-Telegraph-2009-14) Hoult began attending auditions and at the age of five was cast in the 1996 drama *[Intimate Relations](/wiki/Intimate_Relations_(1996_film) "Intimate Relations (1996 film)")*, his first feature-film role. He later appeared in the television programmes *[Casualty](/wiki/Casualty_(TV_series) "Casualty (TV series)")*, *[Silent Witness](/wiki/Silent_Witness "Silent Witness")*, *[The Bill](/wiki/The_Bill "The Bill")*, *[Judge John Deed](/wiki/Judge_John_Deed "Judge John Deed")*, and *[Doctors](/wiki/Doctors_(2000_TV_series) "Doctors (2000 TV series)")*, among others. Hoult initially treated acting as a hobby rather than a potential career option; in a March 2009 interview with *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, he said he was not "in love with it ... I just enjoyed it. It was like playing for a football team. When you got a part it was great. And meeting new people. It was an exciting new world."[[14]](#cite_note-The-Daily-Telegraph-2009-14)

Hoult's next feature-film appearance came at the age of eleven, in [Chris](/wiki/Chris_Weitz "Chris Weitz") and [Paul Weitz](/wiki/Paul_Weitz_(filmmaker) "Paul Weitz (filmmaker)")'s 2002 comedy-drama film *[About a Boy](/wiki/About_a_Boy_(film) "About a Boy (film)")*.[[16]](#cite_note-boyrt-16) Hoult was initially reluctant to audition for the role as the casting process was a lengthy one and interfered with his schooling. He nonetheless decided to participate in the early rounds of auditions and was eventually cast in the role of Marcus, a "woolly-hatted, oddball son of a suicidal, hippy-ish single mother, [who] gets bullied horribly at school".[[17]](#cite_note-davidthomas-17) *About a Boy* was a commercial success, grossing more than $130 million worldwide and receiving praise by film critics.[[16]](#cite_note-boyrt-16)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Hoult's portrayal of a lonely schoolboy was well-received; David Thomas, writing for *The Daily Telegraph*, attributed the film's appeal and success to Hoult's performance.[[17]](#cite_note-davidthomas-17) By the time the film was released, Hoult had left his junior school in [Arborfield](/wiki/Arborfield "Arborfield") and began attending Sylvia Young Theatre School in London. He said the change was difficult; his time there was short and he preferred attending a regular school. He still did not want to pursue acting as a profession and at 14 he left Sylvia Young Theatre School in favour of Ranelagh School.

Hoult starred in [Richard E. Grant](/wiki/Richard_E._Grant "Richard E. Grant")'s semi-autobiographical film *[Wah-Wah](/wiki/Wah-Wah_(film) "Wah-Wah (film)")* (2005) as Ralph Compton, a boy who is forced to deal with the disintegration of his family. The film, set in [Swaziland](/wiki/Swaziland "Swaziland") during the 1960s, chronicles the waning years of the [Swaziland Protectorate](/wiki/History_of_Eswatini "History of Eswatini").[[19]](#cite_note-The-Guardian-2014-19) Hoult made his debut in Hollywood with [Gore Verbinski](/wiki/Gore_Verbinski "Gore Verbinski")'s film *[The Weather Man](/wiki/The_Weather_Man "The Weather Man")* (2005) as the son of a television weather presenter undergoing a [mid-life crisis](/wiki/Mid-life_crisis "Mid-life crisis"). The film and Hoult's performance went largely unnoticed.[[20]](#cite_note-The-Guardian-2014a-20)[[8]](#cite_note-fisher-8) Both *Wah-Wah* and *The Weather Man* performed poorly at the box office.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### 2006–2010: *Skins* and West End debut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2006–2010: Skins and West End debut")]

[![A young, blue-eyed, Caucasian man with short, dark hair wearing a dark-blue jacket, a white shirt, and a black tie against a blurred background of yellow-green tree foliage.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Nicholas_Hoult_2009.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Hoult_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Hoult_2009.jpg)

Hoult at the 2009 [Santa Barbara International Film Festival](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_International_Film_Festival "Santa Barbara International Film Festival")

Hoult was a student at [Sixth Form College Farnborough](/wiki/Sixth_Form_College_Farnborough "Sixth Form College Farnborough") in 2006 when he was cast in the lead role of the television teenage-drama *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")*. He was initially sceptical of his ability to play [Tony Stonem](/wiki/Tony_Stonem "Tony Stonem"), a manipulative, egocentric anti-hero, and identified more closely with the supporting character Sid.[[8]](#cite_note-fisher-8) The programme was a success and ran for seven series, only two of which Hoult appeared in. His performance was well received; the character was popular, and Hoult garnered widespread attention.[[23]](#cite_note-jeffery-23) *Skins* won the [British Academy of Film and Television Arts](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts") (BAFTA) Philip Audience Award, and Hoult was nominated for the [Golden Nymph Award](/wiki/Golden_Nymph_Award "Golden Nymph Award") for Best Actor in a Drama Series.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Critic Elliott David lauded Hoult for his performance in a 2016 retrospective review, and wrote that he "maintain[ed] the inexplicable core of his character throughout".[[25]](#cite_note-David-2015-25) During his time on *Skins*, Hoult felt overwhelmed by the attention he received and considered quitting acting altogether at one point. Instead, he left school at the end of *Skins*' first series and chose to focus solely on acting.[[10]](#cite_note-Adel-2011-10)

Hoult briefly appeared as Stefan Fredman in the pilot episode of the British television series *[Wallander](/wiki/Wallander_(UK_TV_series) "Wallander (UK TV series)")*.[[26]](#cite_note-wallander-26) He later made his [West End theatre](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") debut as Mark, the protagonist in [William Sutcliffe](/wiki/William_Sutcliffe "William Sutcliffe")'s coming-of-age play *New Boy*; the production premiered at [Trafalgar Studios](/wiki/Trafalgar_Studios "Trafalgar Studios") and had record-breaking ticket sales, which was mostly attributed to Hoult's popularity among viewers of *Skins*.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-gardner-28)[[29]](#cite_note-dominic-29) The play was staged for a week in March 2009 because Hoult had committed to a part in the fantasy-adventure film *[Clash of the Titans](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film) "Clash of the Titans (2010 film)")* (2010), filming for which was scheduled for mid-2010. Hoult's performance as Mark, a "ferociously bright and articulate but sexually confused sixth-former" received mixed responses from critics. Dominic Cavendish of *The Daily Telegraph* wrote that his performance was persuasive, but Lyn Gardner of *The Guardian* found him average and highlighted his inability to bring out the "unresolved sexual tension beneath [the] banter".[[28]](#cite_note-gardner-28)[[29]](#cite_note-dominic-29) *[Clash of the Titans](/wiki/Clash_of_the_Titans_(2010_film) "Clash of the Titans (2010 film)")* was panned by critics but was a success at the box office, grossing nearly $500,000,000 worldwide.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Hoult next appeared in [Tom Ford](/wiki/Tom_Ford "Tom Ford")'s *[A Single Man](/wiki/A_Single_Man "A Single Man")* (2009), after the actor originally cast in the role of Kenny Potter left the film a few days before filming commenced. Hoult had previously shown interest in the project and had sent a recorded audition tape; he was eventually chosen for the role of Kenny, a homosexual college student who helps a college professor deal with his grief.[[31]](#cite_note-hicklin-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Variety-2010-32) *A Single Man* was variously described by media outlets as the first adult role for Hoult, who described Kenny as a "spontaneous" character not simply defined by his sexuality.[[32]](#cite_note-Variety-2010-32)[[33]](#cite_note-russnow-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Because the role was his first as an American character, Hoult worked on his [accent](/wiki/American_accent "American accent"); Sukhdev Sandhu of *The Daily Telegraph* noted Ford's choice of casting British actors as Americans (Hoult and [Matthew Goode](/wiki/Matthew_Goode "Matthew Goode")) and vice versa ([Julianne Moore](/wiki/Julianne_Moore "Julianne Moore")).[[35]](#cite_note-eisenman-35)[[36]](#cite_note-sandhu-36) *A Single Man* opened to widespread acclaim despite reservations from critics about Ford's directorial abilities; it was a box-office success.[[37]](#cite_note-pulver-37)[[38]](#cite_note-rose-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) The film earned Hoult a nomination for the [BAFTA Rising Star Award](/wiki/BAFTA_Rising_Star_Award "BAFTA Rising Star Award") at the [2010 ceremony](/wiki/63rd_British_Academy_Film_Awards "63rd British Academy Film Awards").[[40]](#cite_note-orange-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) In 2010, Hoult voiced the character of Elliot in [Lionhead Studios](/wiki/Lionhead_Studios "Lionhead Studios")' [action role-playing game](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game") *[Fable III](/wiki/Fable_III "Fable III")* (2010).[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### 2011–2016: Commercial success with *X-Men* and *Mad Max*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2011–2016: Commercial success with X-Men and Mad Max")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Nicholas_Hoult%2C_2013.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Hoult%2C_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Hoult,_2013.jpg)

Hoult promoting *[X-Men: Days of Future Past](/wiki/X-Men:_Days_of_Future_Past "X-Men: Days of Future Past")* at the 2013 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con")

Hoult was cast as [Nux](/wiki/Nux_(Mad_Max_character) "Nux (Mad Max character)") in [George Miller](/wiki/George_Miller_(director) "George Miller (director)")'s action film *[Mad Max: Fury Road](/wiki/Mad_Max:_Fury_Road "Mad Max: Fury Road")*; the project spent several years in [development hell](/wiki/Development_hell "Development hell") because plans for a fourth film in the [*Mad Max* franchise](/wiki/Mad_Max_(franchise) "Mad Max (franchise)") encountered financial difficulties.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Filming was planned for mid-2010, but heavy rain caused severe delays during pre-production in Australia.[[25]](#cite_note-David-2015-25) With no other immediate commitments, Hoult began to look for other prospects. He was eventually cast in the role of [Hank McCoy / Beast](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)") for the [*X-Men* film series](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)") owing to his ability to play somebody "gentle with a capability of being fierce".[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Before filming began on the 2011 [Matthew Vaughn](/wiki/Matthew_Vaughn "Matthew Vaughn")-directed instalment *[X-Men: First Class](/wiki/X-Men:_First_Class "X-Men: First Class")*, a prequel to the franchise's earlier films, Hoult familiarised himself with his character; he said he "formulated [his] own version of the Beast" and took inspiration from [Kelsey Grammer](/wiki/Kelsey_Grammer "Kelsey Grammer")'s performance in the previous *X-Men: The Last Stand* because he wanted to emulate Grammer's charm and eloquence. Hoult learned to speak in a dialect similar to Grammer's without trying to imitate it.[[46]](#cite_note-46) He also underwent physical training and gained weight to better suit his character.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The film, which was widely praised by critics for its script and performances, performed moderately well at the box office, collecting about $353 million against a production budget of $160 million.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Although it was the lowest-ranked production in the entire series in terms of revenue, Chris Aronson of [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") deemed it "an excellent start to a new chapter of the franchise".[[50]](#cite_note-50)

*Mad Max: Fury Road* was eventually filmed in 2012 in the [Namibian desert](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia"). Miller had conceived Nux, a terminally ill slave, as a "quasi kamikaze pilot"; Hoult said of his character; "he's very enthusiastic and committed and affectionate but also kind of clumsy".[[51]](#cite_note-shoard-51) Hoult shaved his head and followed a strict diet because his role required him to lose a lot of weight.[[52]](#cite_note-calhoun-52) He also talked about performing stunts in the film, describing the entire experience as "scary",[[53]](#cite_note-hill-53) but favourably compared the stunt crew and Miller's choice to incorporate real action sequences instead of using a [green screen](/wiki/Green_screen "Green screen"), saying it made the performance more believable because the actors are placed in a real situation.[[53]](#cite_note-hill-53) *Fury Road* opened to critical acclaim on 14 May 2015 and grossed more than $378 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing film in the *Mad Max* franchise. The technical aspects and stunt sequences drew particular praise from film critics and it was credited for reviving interest in the series.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Shalini Langer noted that while the real hero of the film was [Charlize Theron](/wiki/Charlize_Theron "Charlize Theron"), "[Hoult] is the closest in the acting department ... as Joe's 'war boy' chasing imagined glory".[[56]](#cite_note-56) Hoult's "fabulously unhinged" performance was also praised by [Robbie Collin](/wiki/Robbie_Collin "Robbie Collin") of *The Daily Telegraph*.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Also in 2012, Hoult was included in the [*Forbes* 30 under 30](/wiki/Forbes_30_under_30 "Forbes 30 under 30") list.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

[![A young, Caucasian man with short, dark hair and facial stubble wearing a black shirt speaks into a microphone against a grey background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/SDCC_2015_-_Nicholas_Hoult_%2819752716152%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-SDCC_2015_-_Nicholas_Hoult_%2819752716152%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:SDCC_2015_-_Nicholas_Hoult_(19752716152)_(cropped).jpg)

Hoult promoting *[X-Men: Apocalypse](/wiki/X-Men:_Apocalypse "X-Men: Apocalypse")* at the 2015 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con")

In 2013, Hoult had starring roles in two major films; he first played a [zombie](/wiki/Zombie "Zombie") named R in [Jonathan Levine](/wiki/Jonathan_Levine "Jonathan Levine")'s [romantic comedy](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy") *[Warm Bodies](/wiki/Warm_Bodies_(film) "Warm Bodies (film)")*, which was released on 1 February.[[59]](#cite_note-59) An adaptation of [Isaac Marion](/wiki/Isaac_Marion "Isaac Marion")'s [novel of the same name](/wiki/Warm_Bodies "Warm Bodies"), the film is presented from [point of view](/wiki/Point_of_view_(literature) "Point of view (literature)") of the central character, mostly through narration. Levine said he had difficulties finding a suitable actor to play R until he met Hoult, who was attracted to the project—which he described as "much more than a horror movie" owing to the use of multiple pop culture and literary allusions—and even more so to the role which "bowled [me] over". Hoult said he drew inspiration from *[Edward Scissorhands](/wiki/Edward_Scissorhands "Edward Scissorhands")* (1990) because he thought the central characters in both films share the same difficulties.[[60]](#cite_note-60) To prepare for the role of a zombie, Hoult and the other actors practised with [Cirque du Soleil](/wiki/Cirque_du_Soleil "Cirque du Soleil") performers; he said of the experience; "we would take our shoes off in a dance studio ... kind of grow out of the wall and make our bodies feel very heavy".[[61]](#cite_note-61) The film garnered positive response from critics and audiences.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Ben Kendrick praised Hoult for the restraint in a potentially [Razzie](/wiki/Razzie "Razzie")-worthy role: "[he] brings a lot of life to R without stepping too far in the other direction [and although his] zombie mannerisms may come across a bit forced but, overall, his memorable moments outnumber (and outweigh) the awkward ones."[[63]](#cite_note-63) Hoult was also described as "an extremely appealing actor, [who] is charm personified" in his role of the living-dead.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Hoult's next film, [Bryan Singer](/wiki/Bryan_Singer "Bryan Singer")'s 2013 fantasy adventure *[Jack the Giant Slayer](/wiki/Jack_the_Giant_Slayer "Jack the Giant Slayer")*, failed at the box office and received mixed response from critics. He played [the eponymous hero](/wiki/Jack_(hero) "Jack (hero)") in the film, which is based on the British fairy tales "[Jack the Giant Killer](/wiki/Jack_the_Giant_Killer "Jack the Giant Killer")" and "[Jack and the Beanstalk](/wiki/Jack_and_the_Beanstalk "Jack and the Beanstalk")".[[65]](#cite_note-65) Hoult's performance was poorly received by film critics Mary Pols, [Justin Chang](/wiki/Justin_Chang "Justin Chang") and [Richard Roeper](/wiki/Richard_Roeper "Richard Roeper"); Pols was critical of his "disconcerting" imitation of Hugh Grant and the other two dismissed him and his character as "bland" and "boring", respectively.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Hoult then appeared in [Jake Paltrow](/wiki/Jake_Paltrow "Jake Paltrow")'s science-fiction film *[Young Ones](/wiki/Young_Ones_(film) "Young Ones (film)")* (2014). Set in a dystopian future where water is scarce, the film had Hoult play Flem Lever, a young man who is trying to claim the land owned by the film's central character Ernest Holm.[[69]](#cite_note-Rotten-Tomatoes-69) Hoult thought the role was unlike any of his previous work and said his character's questionable choices throughout the film intrigued him. Hoult read novels written by [S. E. Hinton](/wiki/S._E._Hinton "S. E. Hinton") to prepare for the role. The film was shot in a deserted location in South Africa; Hoult said filming in the hot weather conditions was difficult but the "beautiful" scenery helped to tell the story better.[[70]](#cite_note-kemmerle-70) He said it also made him more conscious of environmental concerns.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The film premiered at the [2014 Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/2014_Sundance_Film_Festival "2014 Sundance Film Festival") and was met with mixed responses.[[70]](#cite_note-kemmerle-70)[[72]](#cite_note-72) Commentators highlighted the film's standout scenery but were critical of its plot.[[69]](#cite_note-Rotten-Tomatoes-69) Keith Uhlich of *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* said Hoult was a poor fit for the story's "stoically retrograde machismo".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Equals_02_%2821312849848%29.jpg/250px-Equals_02_%2821312849848%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Equals_02_(21312849848).jpg)

Hoult with [Kristen Stewart](/wiki/Kristen_Stewart "Kristen Stewart") and [Drake Doremus](/wiki/Drake_Doremus "Drake Doremus") at the premiere of *[Equals](/wiki/Equals_(film) "Equals (film)")* during the [2015 Toronto International Film Festival](/wiki/2015_Toronto_International_Film_Festival "2015 Toronto International Film Festival")

Hoult reprised his role as [Beast](/wiki/Beast_(Marvel_Comics) "Beast (Marvel Comics)") in Bryan Singer's *[X-Men: Days of Future Past](/wiki/X-Men:_Days_of_Future_Past "X-Men: Days of Future Past")*, his other release of 2014. Hoult said playing the character was a freeing experience for him and that it was "fun to suddenly be able to break loose ... when you're wearing the makeup ... you can perform big ... you get to have two very different techniques, performances." He also said the lengthy make-up procedure could last up to three and a half hours.[[74]](#cite_note-74) *X-Men: Days of Future Past* earned more than $747 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing film in the series and in Hoult's career at that time.[[75]](#cite_note-mojoxmen-75)

In 2015, Hoult had three other releases—the [feature film adaptation](/wiki/Dark_Places_(2015_film) "Dark Places (2015 film)") of [Gillian Flynn](/wiki/Gillian_Flynn "Gillian Flynn")'s mystery novel *[Dark Places](/wiki/Dark_Places_(Flynn_novel) "Dark Places (Flynn novel)")*; [Owen Harris](/wiki/Owen_Harris "Owen Harris")' [dark comedy](/wiki/Dark_comedy "Dark comedy") *[Kill Your Friends](/wiki/Kill_Your_Friends_(film) "Kill Your Friends (film)")*, based on the 2008 novel of the same name; and *[Equals](/wiki/Equals_(film) "Equals (film)")*, a dystopian, science-fiction [romantic drama](/wiki/Romantic_drama "Romantic drama") directed by [Drake Doremus](/wiki/Drake_Doremus "Drake Doremus")—all of which were critical failures and rank among the lowest-grossing films of his career.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) Response to Hoult's performance in *Equals* was relatively better;[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) Peter Travers called him and his co-star [Kristen Stewart](/wiki/Kristen_Stewart "Kristen Stewart") "quietly devastating", and Katie Walsh, writing for *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*, said the duo were "finely matched both in their androgynous beauty and in their performances of a repressed humanity".[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) Despite doubts about his contract with the franchise, Hoult returned for the 2016 film *[X-Men: Apocalypse](/wiki/X-Men:_Apocalypse "X-Men: Apocalypse")*. Upon release, the film became the third-ranked *X-Men* film in terms of worldwide box office collections, earning about $540 million.[[75]](#cite_note-mojoxmen-75) It was also a top-grossing production outside the United States.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### 2017–2019: Biographical and independent films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2017–2019: Biographical and independent films")]

[![A caucasian man in a blue sweatshirt looking away from the camera.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Current_War_07_%2837090947661%29.jpg/250px-Current_War_07_%2837090947661%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Current_War_07_(37090947661).jpg)

Hoult at an event for *[The Current War](/wiki/The_Current_War "The Current War")* in 2017

The action film *[Collide](/wiki/Collide_(2016_film) "Collide (2016 film)")*, in which Hoult starred as a drug dealer, was released in the United States in February 2017 to a poor response from audiences and critics.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-collidebo-84) The film garnered negative reviews; its dismal box office performance was attributed to poor marketing and multiple delays caused by the 2015 [chapter 11 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_11_bankruptcy "Chapter 11 bankruptcy") of its production company [Relativity Media](/wiki/Relativity_Media "Relativity Media").[[85]](#cite_note-collidert-85) *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*' Scott Mendelson analysed the film's failure and said Hoult did not necessarily have enough "star power" to draw audiences.[[86]](#cite_note-Mendelson-2017-86) He highlighted the [misogyny](/wiki/Misogyny "Misogyny") and sense of entitlement in the entertainment industry, writing that director [Eran Creevy](/wiki/Eran_Creevy "Eran Creevy") and Hoult would get better offers despite the failure of films like *Collide*, as opposed to the women and other [minority groups](/wiki/Minority_group "Minority group"), who are either ignored or stereotyped.[[86]](#cite_note-Mendelson-2017-86) Responses to Hoult's next film, the romance drama *[Newness](/wiki/Newness "Newness")*, were more enthusiastic.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The production had its world premiere at the [2017 Sundance Film Festival](/wiki/2017_Sundance_Film_Festival "2017 Sundance Film Festival"); it stars Hoult as one half of a Los Angeles-based couple who meet through [online dating](/wiki/Online_dating "Online dating") and begin an [open relationship](/wiki/Open_relationship "Open relationship").[[88]](#cite_note-hoffman-88) Drake Doremus, the film's director, said Hoult's role was unlike his previous work; "a very complex and emotionally mature performance that we haven't seen yet".[[89]](#cite_note-89)

Hoult starred in a series of biographical and historical films in 2017; he said he preferred playing characters that might help him improve as an actor and that the "actors I look up to started doing their best work in their early 30s and I'll be hitting that age ... I'm just trying to learn".[[90]](#cite_note-barlow-90) He portrayed American author [J. D. Salinger](/wiki/J._D._Salinger "J. D. Salinger") in [Danny Strong](/wiki/Danny_Strong "Danny Strong")'s *[Rebel in the Rye](/wiki/Rebel_in_the_Rye "Rebel in the Rye")*, which chronicles Salinger's life from his youth to the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") era and the years preceding the publication of his debut novel, *[The Catcher in the Rye](/wiki/The_Catcher_in_the_Rye "The Catcher in the Rye")*.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Hoult auditioned for the role because he was intrigued by the film's script and Salinger's enigmatic personality; "I didn't know he fought in the second world war and landed on [D-Day](/wiki/D-Day "D-Day"): ... had intermittent [PTSD](/wiki/PTSD "PTSD") or that he became interested in [Vedanta](/wiki/Vedanta "Vedanta") philosophy and meditated and did yoga."[[90]](#cite_note-barlow-90) To prepare for the role, Hoult read *The Catcher in the Rye* and biographies about Salinger. Hoult said the biggest challenge was to get a real understanding of Salinger's character; "everyone has an idea of [Salinger] in their mind ... you're creating a character that people have very strong feelings about. You can't prove to be right or wrong through impressions."[[92]](#cite_note-92) *Rebel in the Rye* opened to a poor response from film critics.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Carson Lund of *[Slant](/wiki/Slant_(magazine) "Slant (magazine)")* was largely unimpressed by Hoult's "feeble" performance and his inability to "reinvest the character with the complexities lost in the story's programmatic telling".[[94]](#cite_note-94) [RogerEbert.com](/wiki/RogerEbert.com "RogerEbert.com")'s Matt Fagerholm wrote that although Hoult was capable of illuminating the insecurities and fixations of his character, he is never "quite believable as Salinger". Fagerholm ascribed the failure to the script, which left the character's key motivations "frustratingly muddled".[[95]](#cite_note-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/NicholasHoultTolkien2.jpg/250px-NicholasHoultTolkien2.jpg)](/wiki/File:NicholasHoultTolkien2.jpg)

Hoult at the 2019 [Montclair Film Festival](/wiki/Montclair_Film_Festival "Montclair Film Festival")

Hoult co-starred in *[The Current War](/wiki/The_Current_War "The Current War")*, a dramatisation of the feud between electrical pioneers [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") and [George Westinghouse](/wiki/George_Westinghouse "George Westinghouse"). Hoult was cast in the role of [Nikola Tesla](/wiki/Nikola_Tesla "Nikola Tesla"), for which he grew a moustache and attended science lessons about [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism") and dynamos.[[90]](#cite_note-barlow-90)[[96]](#cite_note-holly-96) He lost weight for his role by following a strict diet.[[97]](#cite_note-mclean-97) Response to *The Current War* was mixed; [David Ehrlich](/wiki/David_Ehrlich "David Ehrlich") of *[IndieWire](/wiki/IndieWire "IndieWire")* described Hoult's performance as a tribute to [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), who had previously played Tesla in *[The Prestige](/wiki/The_Prestige "The Prestige")* (2006).[[98]](#cite_note-98) In a departure from biographical dramas, Hoult then starred as an American soldier in *[Sand Castle](/wiki/Sand_Castle_(film) "Sand Castle (film)")*, a production he described as a very different war film "in terms of the pacing and the emotion ... very under the surface, that futility-of-war idea".[[97]](#cite_note-mclean-97) He recalled the filming experience in the Jordanian military bases practising clearing procedures: "we put on these masks, get given these guns, are put inside this pitch-black house ... try and hunt down these bad guys hiding inside. You're in all the gear ... the adrenaline starts pumping."[[97]](#cite_note-mclean-97) Released on Netflix in 2017, the film garnered mixed reviews.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

In *[The Favourite](/wiki/The_Favourite_(2018_film) "The Favourite (2018 film)")* (2018), a critically acclaimed period drama about [Anne, Queen of Great Britain](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), Hoult played the supporting role of [Robert Harley, 1st Earl of Oxford and Earl Mortimer](/wiki/Robert_Harley,_1st_Earl_of_Oxford_and_Earl_Mortimer "Robert Harley, 1st Earl of Oxford and Earl Mortimer").[[100]](#cite_note-100) He next voiced [Fiver](/wiki/Fiver_(Watership_Down) "Fiver (Watership Down)") in the animated [television miniseries](/wiki/Watership_Down_(2018_TV_series) "Watership Down (2018 TV series)") adaptation of [Richard Adams](/wiki/Richard_Adams "Richard Adams")' 1972 novel *[Watership Down](/wiki/Watership_Down "Watership Down")*.[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) In 2019, Hoult portrayed author [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien") in the biopic *[Tolkien](/wiki/Tolkien_(film) "Tolkien (film)")*.[[103]](#cite_note-103) He next reprised his role as Hank McCoy in the X-Men film *[Dark Phoenix](/wiki/Dark_Phoenix_(film) "Dark Phoenix (film)")*.[[104]](#cite_note-104) Both *Tolkien* and *Dark Phoenix* were poorly received.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106)

### 2020–present: Antagonistic characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2020–present: Antagonistic characters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Nicholas_Hoult_at_MEGACON_Orlando_2025.png/250px-Nicholas_Hoult_at_MEGACON_Orlando_2025.png)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Hoult_at_MEGACON_Orlando_2025.png)

Nicholas Hoult at [MegaCon](/wiki/MegaCon "MegaCon") in 2025

The following year, Hoult began starring as [Peter III of Russia](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Russia "Peter III of Russia") in the [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") comedy-drama series *[The Great](/wiki/The_Great_(TV_series) "The Great (TV series)")*.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The series and his performance received critical acclaim.[[108]](#cite_note-108) He was nominated for two [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Television_Series_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Television Series Musical or Comedy") and a [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Comedy Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Lead_Actor_in_a_Comedy_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Comedy Series").[[109]](#cite_note-Sherman-2022-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) In 2021, Hoult appeared in a villainous role in the thriller *[Those Who Wish Me Dead](/wiki/Those_Who_Wish_Me_Dead "Those Who Wish Me Dead")*.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Hoult starred in the black comedy film *[The Menu](/wiki/The_Menu_(2022_film) "The Menu (2022 film)")* in 2022. It received positive reviews.[[112]](#cite_note-112) Hoult next played the [titular role](/wiki/Renfield "Renfield") in the comedy-horror film *[Renfield](/wiki/Renfield_(film) "Renfield (film)").*[[113]](#cite_note-113) In June 2023, he was invited to join the [Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Motion_Picture_Arts_and_Sciences "Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences") in the actors branch.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

In 2024, Hoult voiced [Jon Arbuckle](/wiki/Jon_Arbuckle "Jon Arbuckle") in the animated film *[The Garfield Movie](/wiki/The_Garfield_Movie "The Garfield Movie")*.[[115]](#cite_note-115) That same year, he also starred in the crime thriller *[The Order](/wiki/The_Order_(2024_film) "The Order (2024 film)")* (2024), directed by [Justin Kurzel](/wiki/Justin_Kurzel "Justin Kurzel"), and the courtroom drama *[Juror #2](/wiki/Juror_No._2 "Juror No. 2")* (2024), directed by [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood").[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) Hoult also starred as [Thomas Hutter](/wiki/Thomas_Hutter "Thomas Hutter") in *[Nosferatu](/wiki/Nosferatu_(2024_film) "Nosferatu (2024 film)")*, written and directed by [Robert Eggers](/wiki/Robert_Eggers "Robert Eggers").[[118]](#cite_note-118) Hoult appeared as [Lex Luthor](/wiki/Lex_Luthor "Lex Luthor") in *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_(2025_film) "Superman (2025 film)")* (2025) from director [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn").[[119]](#cite_note-119) Hoult described Luthor as obsessive, determined, and relentless, and wanted the character to feel like a credible threat to Superman.[[120]](#cite_note-Hickson-2024-120) Gunn took inspiration for the character from [Brian Azzarello](/wiki/Brian_Azzarello "Brian Azzarello")'s limited comic book series *[Lex Luthor: Man of Steel](/wiki/Lex_Luthor:_Man_of_Steel "Lex Luthor: Man of Steel")* (2005) as well as [Jerry Siegel](/wiki/Jerry_Siegel "Jerry Siegel")'s "reckless scientific genius" version of Luthor in comic books from the 1950s and 1960s.[[121]](#cite_note-Brown-2025-121)[[122]](#cite_note-Romano-2025-122)

Hoult will star in *[How to Rob a Bank](/wiki/How_to_Rob_a_Bank_(2026_film) "How to Rob a Bank (2026 film)")* directed by filmmaker [David Leitch](/wiki/David_Leitch "David Leitch"), with a script by Mark Bianculli, which tells the story of a bank robbery for [Amazon MGM Studios](/wiki/Amazon_MGM_Studios "Amazon MGM Studios"). It is set for release on 4 September 2026.[[123]](#cite_note-123) He will also star in *[Cry to Heaven](/wiki/Cry_to_Heaven_(film) "Cry to Heaven (film)")*, directed by [Tom Ford](/wiki/Tom_Ford "Tom Ford").[[124]](#cite_note-124)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Nicholas_Hoult_%2847519169471%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Hoult_%2847519169471%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Hoult_(47519169471)_(cropped).jpg)

Hoult at the 2019 [WonderCon](/wiki/WonderCon "WonderCon")

Hoult divides his time between [London](/wiki/London "London") and [Huntington Beach, California](/wiki/Huntington_Beach,_California "Huntington Beach, California").[[125]](#cite_note-125) In his free time, he plays [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") and [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf")[[126]](#cite_note-Abramovitch-2021-126) and trains in [jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Jujutsu "Jujutsu") and [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing").[[126]](#cite_note-Abramovitch-2021-126) He is also an avid fan of [Grand Prix motorcycle racing](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motorcycle_racing "Grand Prix motorcycle racing") and [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") and has been spotted at various Grand Prix events over the years.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) Hoult graduated from Ferrari's Corso Pilota driving school in 2022 and has competed in multiple [Ferrari Challenge](/wiki/Ferrari_Challenge "Ferrari Challenge") events. In 2024, he won the "Track Attack" at [Watkins Glen](/wiki/Watkins_Glen_International "Watkins Glen International") with his [Ferrari 296 GTB](/wiki/Ferrari_296 "Ferrari 296").[[130]](#cite_note-130)

During the filming of *[X-Men: First Class](/wiki/X-Men:_First_Class "X-Men: First Class")* in 2010, Hoult began dating his co-star [Jennifer Lawrence](/wiki/Jennifer_Lawrence "Jennifer Lawrence"). Their relationship ended around the time they wrapped filming *[X-Men: Days of Future Past](/wiki/X-Men:_Days_of_Future_Past "X-Men: Days of Future Past")* in August 2014.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Hoult is the father of two children with American model Bryana Holly, with whom he had been in a relationship since 12 December 2016.[[96]](#cite_note-holly-96)[[133]](#cite_note-Booth-2023-133) Their first child was born in 2018, and their second in 2022. Hoult called Holly his "wife" at the 2024 Governors Awards on 17 November 2024.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[133]](#cite_note-Booth-2023-133)

In mid-2025, Hoult tore his ACL while playing basketball and had to undergo surgery in October 2025 to fix it.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

## Philanthropy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Hoult&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Philanthropy")]

Hoult is a philanthropist and supports numerous charities; he has been associated with organisations that support children. He was appointed the first [National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children](/wiki/National_Society_for_the_Prevention_of_Cruelty_to_Children "National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children") (NSPCC) Young Person Ambassador, for supporting the charity's activities aimed at children and young people.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Since 2009, he has also been involved with the [Teenage Cancer Trust](/wiki/Teenage_Cancer_Trust "Teenage Cancer Trust"); he continues to visit patients supported by the organisation and has helped promote its awareness campaigns, including the sun-safety campaign "Shunburn".[[137]](#cite_note-137) Hoult designed sweaters for [Save the Children](/wiki/Save_the_Children "Save the Children")'s and Selfish Mother's joint [Christmas Jumper Day](/wiki/Christmas_Jumper_Day "Christmas Jumper Day") campaign. He encouraged customers to buy the festive collection and support the charitable cause, which he thought would bring a "real change to children's lives".[[138]](#cite_note-138) He also donated a pair of shoes, which was auctioned by Small Steps Project, an organisation that helps homeless and malnourished children.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Hoult was inducted into the NSPCC Hall of Fame in 2010 for his contributions to the campaign against child cruelty.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Tolkien_%2847014503684%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Tolkien_%2847014503684%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tolkien_(47014503684)_(cropped).jpg)

Hoult in 2019

Hoult visited [Nairobi](/wiki/Nairobi "Nairobi"), Kenya, as a part of a [Christian Aid](/wiki/Christian_Aid "Christian Aid") project aimed at providing clean water and sanitation. During his stay he met local people and helped clean the locality. He said of his experience; "I met great people making the best of the situation ... it is heart-breaking in many ways to see the living conditions".[[141]](#cite_note-141) Hoult also participated in the [Rickshaw Run](/wiki/Rickshaw_Run "Rickshaw Run") in January 2017, in which participants drove an [auto rickshaw](/wiki/Auto_rickshaw "Auto rickshaw") (also called a *tuk tuk*) for 3,000 kilometres (1,900 mi) across India to raise funds for Teenage Cancer Trust and [World Wide Fund for Nature](/wiki/World_Wide_Fund_for_Nature "World Wide Fund for Nature").[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143) He has also been associated with Jeans for Refugees, a project and fundraising initiative intended to help refugees around the world.[[144]](#cite_note-144) He donated a signed pair of jeans to the organisation; profits from the campaign were donated to the refugee support agency [International Rescue Committee](/wiki/International_Rescue_Committee "International Rescue Committee").[[145]](#cite_note-145)

On 1 August 2020, he participated in the Outreach Project campaign alongside [Charlize Theron](/wiki/Charlize_Theron "Charlize Theron"), where the film *Mad Max: Fury Road* was screened at a drive-in theater in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") to raise funds.[[146]](#cite_note-146) In 2021, for [Save the Children UK](/wiki/Save_the_Children "Save the Children"), he took part in Misan Harriman's campaign to raise awareness about the urgent changes children want to see in the world: "Ban plastic. Reduce pollution."[[147]](#cite_note-147) In June 2022, he participated in [Biotherm](/wiki/Biotherm "Biotherm")'s #BeAWaterLover campaign to raise awareness about ocean preservation and to announce the brand's commitment to becoming *Ocean Positive* by 2030.*[[148]](#cite_note-148)* In both 2022 and 2023, he championed [Armani](/wiki/Armani "Armani")'s Acqua for Life campaign.*[[149]](#cite_note-149)*

In 2025, Hoult was named a [Save the Children](/wiki/Save_the_Children "Save the Children") Ambassador.[[150]](#cite_note-Carson-2025-150) As part of his new role, he visited an after-school workshop in London, where he spoke with children about topics related to his role as [Lex Luthor](/wiki/Lex_Luthor "Lex Luthor") in *Superman* (2025) and promoted values such as compassion and responsibility. As an ambassador, Hoult supports the organisation's initiatives focused on education, health, and child welfare at a global level.[[150]](#cite_note-Carson-2025-150)
